title: Mechanics Infrastructure Separation
use_for: separating domain mechanics from lifecycle, threading, hooks, UI, logging, persistence, and external APIs
designing a domain core that can run behind adapters
preventing infrastructure concerns from becoming domain rules
creating testable mechanics without a full host runtime
do_not_use_for: pure infrastructure design with no domain mechanics
low-level performance tuning without ownership questions
rules: domain mechanics should consume explicit inputs and produce explicit outputs
infrastructure should own lifecycle, external API access, threading, logging, and persistence
adapters may translate external state; they must not hide domain decisions
domain code should not depend on host callbacks, UI controls, reflection probes, or loader details
mutation of external state should pass through a named boundary
procedure: List the domain mechanics and the external forces they depend on.
Classify each dependency as input data, command output, lifecycle signal, configuration, diagnostic, or infrastructure service.
Define pure domain models and state transitions where practical.
Place external API calls behind adapters, probes, facades, or command appliers.
Define ownership of mutation, scheduling, diagnostics, and persistence outside the mechanics.
Verify that the mechanics can be exercised without the full host runtime.

primary_when: the task is mainly about separating domain mechanics from runtime plumbing
the task is designing a new domain core for a host-managed system
must_not_own_when: the task is only about host startup or UI layout
the task does not involve domain behavior

solves: how to keep gameplay or domain mechanics independent from infrastructure
how to avoid lifecycle and adapter leakage into the domain model
how to design testable core logic for hosted runtimes

core_principles: Mechanics decide what should happen; infrastructure decides when and how host systems are touched.
External state should be sampled into stable inputs before mechanics consume it.
External mutation should be represented as commands or scoped operations before it reaches adapters.
Diagnostics observe boundaries; they should not become the boundary.
constraints: Do not let domain mechanics call host APIs directly unless the host API is the domain itself.
Do not hide lifecycle readiness checks inside mechanics calculations.
Do not make UI state the source of truth for domain state.
Do not make logging or diagnostics required for mechanics correctness.
decision_rules: If code reads host state, place it in a probe or adapter unless it is already a stable domain snapshot.
If code mutates host state, route it through a command applier or mutation scope.
If code requires lifecycle readiness, gate it at module/runtime level before invoking mechanics.
If a mechanic cannot be tested without the host runtime, identify which input or mutation boundary is missing.
If an infrastructure concern appears in a domain type, either move it outward or rename the type as infrastructure.

avoid_patterns: UI controls mutating external state directly
reflection probes returning live objects to mechanics
global mutable singletons as domain state
adapters that contain hidden domain policy

recipes.name: Extract mechanics from infrastructure
recipes.when: Use when domain behavior is currently mixed with host APIs, lifecycle, UI, or logging.
steps: Identify the calculation or decision that is the mechanic.
Replace direct external reads with explicit input snapshots.
Replace direct external writes with command outputs or mutation scopes.
Move lifecycle readiness and diagnostics to the caller boundary.
Add a small test or replay path for the mechanic without the host runtime.

pitfalls: Calling an adapter a domain service while it still owns external API calls.
Letting convenience access to host state define the domain model.
Moving code into a new namespace without changing ownership.
Using UI DTOs as domain entities.
review_checklist: Can mechanics run from explicit inputs?
Are external mutations routed through named boundaries?
Are lifecycle and readiness checks outside the mechanics?
Can the domain core be tested without the host runtime?
Are adapters free of hidden domain policy?

references.title: Composition Boundaries
references.interpretation: Use to separate domain mechanics from infrastructure ownership.
references.why: Mechanics and infrastructure need explicit composition boundaries.

references.title: Contracts
references.interpretation: Use for mechanics input, output, and mutation contracts.
references.why: Domain logic should depend on explicit contracts rather than host details.
